description: Essential Latin legal terms, maxims, and phrases with translations and jurisprudential usage

terms.jurisprudential_usage: The doctrine of precedent requiring courts to follow earlier judicial decisions when the same points arise again in litigation.

terms.translation: in the same matter; on the same subject
terms.jurisprudential_usage: Statutes dealing with the same subject matter should be construed together to give effect to the legislative intent.
A person cannot be punished for an act that was not prohibited by law at the time it was committed; principle against ex post facto laws.

terms.latin: actus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea
terms.translation: the act does not make a person guilty unless the mind is also guilty
terms.jurisprudential_usage: Criminal liability requires both a wrongful act (actus reus) and criminal intent (mens rea).
The material evidence that a crime has been committed; the essential elements of an offense.
A final judgment on the merits bars further claims by the parties on the same cause of action.
A matter that is currently before a court and not yet decided.
A proceeding brought by one party without notice to or contestation by any other party.
An act performed without authority; actions exceeding the legal power or authority of a person or institution.
A law governing a specific subject matter prevails over laws governing more general matters (lex generalis).
A later enacted statute takes precedence over an earlier one when they conflict (lex posterior derogat legi priori).
An order with retroactive effect, treating an act as if it had been done at an earlier time.
A writ requiring a person under arrest to be brought before a judge to determine if detention is lawful.
A writ compelling a government official or entity to perform a mandatory duty.
A writ seeking appellate review; discretionary review by a higher court of a lower court's decision.
A proceeding challenging a person's right to hold public office or exercise a franchise.
A person or entity that is not a party to a case but offers information or expertise to assist the court.
The mental state or intent required for criminal liability.
The physical act or unlawful omission that comprises the physical components of a crime.
Evidence sufficient to establish a fact or raise a presumption unless disproved or rebutted.
A doctrine allowing negligence to be inferred from the circumstances without direct evidence.
Existing in fact, whether legally recognized or not; actual rather than legal.
According to law; by right; legitimate; lawful.
Made or done sincerely, honestly, and without fraud or deceit.
With intent to deceive or defraud; dishonestly.
Principle that the buyer purchases at their own risk without warranty unless provided.
An exchange of one thing for another; mutual consideration in contracts.

terms.translation: in the place of a parent
terms.jurisprudential_usage: Having the legal rights, responsibilities, and authority of a parent.
In and of itself; intrinsically; without reference to extrinsic evidence.
An opinion issued by a court as a whole rather than by a single judge.
Legal services provided without charge, especially to indigent clients.
Representing oneself in court without an attorney.
A judicial proceeding conducted in private or in a judge's chambers.
An action taken by a court on its own motion, without a party's request.
A court order requiring a person to appear and testify or produce documents.
The examination of prospective jurors or witnesses to determine their qualifications.
A legal action directed against property rather than against a person.
A legal action directed against a specific person, seeking personal liability.
A law that retroactively criminalizes conduct or increases punishment; constitutionally prohibited.
The current or existing condition; often used in injunction analysis.
Created or done for a particular purpose as necessary; not general.
A new review or trial conducted as if no previous decision had been made.
Used to indicate that a list or statement is not exhaustive.

terms.translation: with necessary changes having been made
terms.jurisprudential_usage: Applying a rule or principle with appropriate modifications.
The legal principle that forms the basis for a court's decision; binding precedent.
A judicial comment made during a decision that is not essential to the outcome; not binding precedent.
The law of the jurisdiction where an event occurred or property is located.
A doctrine allowing a court to dismiss a case when another forum would be more appropriate.
The right or capacity to bring an action or to appear in a court; standing.
By the very nature of the fact; by that very fact or act.
